text: The surgeon dissects fibrosed inflammatory adhesions of the omentum over the gallbladder to expose and identify the gallbladder.

The surgeon continues dissection to expose the bottom of the gallbladder, opening the peritoneum over it. The dissection proceeds laterally towards the right side, separating the peritoneum along the right lateral aspect of the gallbladder to facilitate retraction away from the liver bed.

The surgeon begins dissecting around the cystic duct, revealing stones within it. The cystic artery is visible, and dissection is performed on both the medial and lateral aspects to elevate it away from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon performs dissection behind the gallbladder, revealing the corkscrew-shaped cystic duct folded onto the bottom of the gallbladder. The tissue is carefully dissected to expose the liver bed through the triangle, aiming to establish a clear view.

The surgeon encounters bleeding from a vessel near the cystic duct during dissection. Using cautery, the surgeon controls the bleeding, ensuring the vessel is managed appropriately in relation to the ductal structure.

The surgeon identifies a long, spiraled cystic duct containing multiple stones. A posterior branch of the cystic artery is visible, which is clipped to prevent further bleeding. The surgeon presses on a stone within the cystic duct, causing slight bleeding at the junction with the gallbladder, and applies a proximal clip. A ductotomy is performed to remove stones from the cystic duct.

The surgeon irrigates the area to remove stones from the cystic duct, which are placed above the kidney for later removal. The cystic duct is clipped and divided, and the surgeon prepares to take the cystic artery and detach the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon carefully avoids creating a hole in the gallbladder, which is packed with stones, preventing them from spilling. The gallbladder is placed into a retrieval bag. The surgeon uses an endo-loop to secure the cystic duct.

Other than that, the case went well. The patient was discharged the same day, had their stent removed about a week later. As an outpatient, there were no residual stones in the cystic or the thymobiobuct. I hope you enjoyed it.
